# arXiv 2510.21413

## Core Thesis

Repository-level AI instruction files were still rare in the paper’s analyzed GitHub sample, and the AGENTS.md files that did exist had not converged on a stable template: adoption was low, structure was heterogeneous, and maintenance patterns suggest many files were created once and then left mostly static, while the subset that did receive repeated attention was updated mainly through instruction edits.

## Why It Matters for Sapho

This matters because it cuts against any assumption that agent-facing repository guidance is already a mature, standardized operating layer. The field signal here is early and uneven: most repositories in the analyzed sample had none of the four examined configuration formats, and even within AGENTS.md the recurring topics do not amount to a settled doctrine. For Sapho, that argues for caution in treating current agent-instruction files as robust institutional scaffolding. It also reinforces a doctrine of explicit bounds: observed usage patterns and document conventions are still contingent, sample-bound, and not yet reliable proxies for durable best practice.

## Key Findings

- In the analyzed 10,000-repository subset, only 466 repositories, or 5%, contained at least one of the four examined AI configuration file types: Copilot instructions, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or GEMINI.md.
- The paper’s adoption result is not drawn from the full initial repository pool. The authors reduced 225,582 repositories to 49,311 through filtering, then analyzed a ranked 10,000-repository subset chosen to balance popularity and maturity.
- AGENTS.md files did not exhibit a settled common structure. Conventions and best practices was the most frequent heading category at 50 occurrences, followed by contribution guidelines at 48, architecture and project structure at 47, and build commands at 40, but the files still varied widely in organization and presentation style.
- The source identifies five stylistic modes within AGENTS.md convention sections: descriptive, prescriptive, prohibitive, explanatory, and conditional, reinforcing that recurring content categories coexist with divergent rhetorical patterns.
- AGENTS.md maintenance was often sparse: among 155 files, 77 files, or 50%, were unchanged after the initial commit; 36 files, or 23%, changed once; and 32 files, or 21%, changed between two and seven times.
- Where heavily revised files were examined, instruction work dominated the edits: in 169 annotated commits from files with at least 10 commits, adding instructions appeared 78 times and modifying instructions 59 times.

## Evidence and Findings

- The paper’s strongest empirical adoption result is narrow but clear: only 466 of 10,000 analyzed repositories had any of the four target AI configuration file types, a 5% uptake rate. That supports the conclusion that explicit agent-facing repository configuration was still uncommon in the studied sample, which matters because many discussions of agent tooling imply broader operational normalization than this evidence shows.
- The adoption claim is methodologically bounded twice over. The authors first filtered an initial pool of 225,582 repositories down to 49,311, then selected a ranked 10,000-repository work-in-progress subset balancing popularity and maturity. That means the result supports a claim about a constructed sample rather than GitHub at large, which matters because apparent ecosystem-level conclusions would outrun the study design.
- Within AGENTS.md, repeated topical motifs did appear, but not a stable layout. Conventions and best practices led heading frequency with 50 top-level or second-level headings, alongside contribution guidance at 48, architecture and project structure at 47, and build commands at 40. This supports the conclusion that some content expectations are emerging, but they have not hardened into a shared document structure that would make AGENTS.md a standardized interface.
- The source’s style analysis deepens that heterogeneity result. Convention sections spanned descriptive, prescriptive, prohibitive, explanatory, and conditional modes, indicating that even when repositories addressed similar concerns, they framed them differently. That matters because agent guidance quality is not just about topic coverage; it also depends on how instructions are expressed and constrained.
- Maintenance behavior points to a split pattern rather than a single lifecycle. Half of the 155 AGENTS.md files never changed after the initial commit, while a smaller subset accumulated repeated edits. This supports the conclusion that many projects treat these files as static setup artifacts rather than continuously maintained operational contracts.
- In the subset of files with at least 10 commits, the most common annotated changes were adding instructions (78 instances) and modifying instructions (59 instances). That matters because when repositories do invest in ongoing revision, the work concentrates on instruction content itself rather than on a demonstrated convergence toward one canonical format or governance model.

## Contradictions and Tensions

- The paper reports both recurring structure signals and the absence of an established structure. Conventions and best practices, contribution guidance, architecture, and build commands appear frequently, yet the authors still conclude that AGENTS.md lacks a common organizational template. The tension is that shared concerns are emerging without yielding a shared form.
- The maintenance evidence is mixed rather than uniform. Half of AGENTS.md files were untouched after creation, which suggests weak ongoing stewardship, but the more actively revised subset shows concentrated instruction editing. That means the ecosystem is not simply static; it appears split between dormant documents and files treated as living guidance.
- The structure analysis itself is bounded by exclusions. Fifteen AGENTS.md files created before January 1, 2025 and five headingless files were left out of the heading analysis, so the reported structural heterogeneity is informative but not fully comprehensive across all observed AGENTS.md files.
- Co-occurrence data also complicates simple format narratives. AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md were the most common pair, but only in 25 repositories, which suggests some repositories layer multiple agent-facing documents while overall adoption remains low. The field may be experimenting with overlapping conventions before any one pattern dominates.
- File-length statistics imply another unresolved tension. AGENTS.md averaged 142 lines with a standard deviation of 231, far more dispersed than GEMINI.md at 106 lines with a standard deviation of 65 and more volatile than Copilot instructions at 310 lines with a standard deviation of 127. That spread is consistent with the paper’s heterogeneity claim, but it also suggests that “AGENTS.md” may cover very different operational roles across repositories.

## Mechanism or Bounds

The best-supported explanation is not causal but operational: adoption appears bounded by a still-emergent convention layer rather than a mature standard. The study shows low uptake across four examined file types and substantial variation within AGENTS.md, which together support a view of repository-level agent configuration as early-stage, fragmented practice. The evidence does not establish why uptake was low, why some projects converged on repeated headings, or why some files remained static while others accumulated instruction edits. It supports bounded observations about one ranked 10,000-repository subset and, within AGENTS.md, about observed structural and maintenance patterns rather than a general mechanism for effective context engineering.

## Limits

The findings are sample-bound and format-bound: the paper analyzes a ranked 10,000-repository subset drawn from a filtered pool of 49,311 repositories, and the adoption result covers only four AI configuration file types.
The evidence does not justify ecosystem-wide prevalence claims, causal explanations for low uptake, or stronger claims about what document structure actually produces better agent performance.
The AGENTS.md structure analysis excludes pre-2025 files and headingless files, so the reported pattern of heterogeneity is informative but incomplete.
The commit-category analysis is narrower still, limited to files with at least 10 commits, which means the edit-pattern result should not be read as representative of all AGENTS.md histories.
---
